notes.note: The societates publicanorum were joint-stock companies of equestrian businessmen who bid for state contracts to collect provincial taxes and customs duties; the Bithynian company held the franchise for the province of Bithynia et Pontus.

The ordo publicanorum (order of tax-farmers) was a powerful sub-group of the equestrian order; Cicero had famously defended their interests in the speech Pro Lege Manilia (66 BC) and cultivated their political support throughout his career.

notes.anchor: composed of the rest of the companies
notes.note: This cryptic phrase indicates the Bithynian company was a consortium formed from members of several other publicani companies, making it the single largest block of the citizen body among the societates.

notes.anchor: Publius Rupilius the son of Publius, of the Menenian tribe, who is the company's manager
notes.note: The magister was the elected managing director who handled the company's day-to-day operations in Rome; the tribal designation (Menenia) is part of his formal Roman citizen identification, not a rank.

notes.anchor: how much such a matter falls within a quaestor's competence
notes.note: The quaestor was the junior magistrate assigned to a provincial governor, with responsibility for the treasury and financial administration; in that capacity he had direct dealing with the publicani collecting taxes in his province.
